method: Primary-source synthesis from official framework, platform, and tooling documentation. Deep research expansion covers AI/LLM toolchains, agentic frameworks, ML/data tools, vector databases, IaC, BaaS, dev tooling, and production comparison guidance with explicit decision rules and maintenance cadence.

stacks.name: React
strengths: Component model stays effective for highly interactive interfaces.
Large ecosystem and broad staffing availability.
Fits both simple SPA shells and framework-driven SSR stacks.
tradeoffs: React alone is a UI library, not a full delivery architecture.
State, routing, data fetching, and build choices still need platform decisions.
best_for: Interactive product UIs with non-trivial client state.
Teams that want a common UI model across multiple delivery options.
avoid_when: A simple server-rendered app can be handled faster with a batteries-included framework.
The team needs strong architectural defaults more than flexibility.

stacks.name: Vite
strengths: Fast local dev server with native-module oriented workflow.
Lean default for frontend apps that do not need framework-level full-stack features.
Plugin model fits mixed frontend stacks and monorepos.
tradeoffs: It solves build/runtime ergonomics, not backend-for-frontend concerns.
SSR and production architecture still require extra framework or platform design.
best_for: Pure frontend apps with separate APIs.
Design systems, admin tools, dashboards, and embedded product UIs.
avoid_when: The app needs integrated routing, server rendering, and data mutation conventions.
The team wants file-system routing and opinionated deployment defaults.

stacks.name: Next.js
strengths: Integrated routing, layouts, loading states, and API route handlers.
Good fit when frontend and backend-for-frontend logic should live together.
Supports mixed rendering strategies per route instead of one global model.
tradeoffs: Complexity rises quickly when Server Components, caching, and route handlers mix without clear boundaries.
A poor fit if the team wants a thin frontend over a separately owned API platform.
best_for: Content-heavy or product-heavy web apps that need SEO and responsive perceived performance.
BFF patterns where route-level data loading, auth, and rendering should be colocated.
avoid_when: The frontend should stay deployment-independent from the core backend.
The application is mostly an internal SPA where SSR adds more cost than value.

stacks.name: Django
strengths: Strong batteries-included platform with ORM, admin, forms, auth, and security features.
Excellent when the application benefits from tight coupling between data model, admin operations, and web workflows.
Mature deployment, testing, and validation surfaces.
tradeoffs: Heavier than an API-first microservice framework for narrow services.
Can become rigid if the team is trying to force highly decoupled service boundaries from day one.
best_for: Business systems, admin-rich products, operational consoles, and content-heavy web apps.
Teams that want one coherent framework for data, auth, forms, and templating.
avoid_when: The product is mostly a lightweight async API with minimal server-rendered UI.
The architecture requires independently deployed services with minimal shared framework assumptions.

stacks.name: FastAPI
strengths: Type-hint driven request validation and OpenAPI generation reduce API drift.
Good fit for async APIs, typed contracts, and service boundaries around automation or ML workloads.
Lower ceremony than larger batteries-included frameworks.
tradeoffs: Teams still need to choose their own ORM, migrations, background strategy, and admin story.
Async support does not remove the need for explicit durability and job design.
best_for: API-first services, automation backends, data services, and platform control planes.
Python stacks where typed contracts matter more than server-rendered UI.
avoid_when: The product needs a full integrated admin/content/forms platform immediately.
Long-running business workflows are being modeled as request handlers instead of durable jobs or workflows.

stacks.name: NestJS
strengths: Provides architectural structure for Node teams that need clear modules, providers, and test seams.
Good compromise between raw Express/Fastify flexibility and enterprise-grade conventions.
Works well for larger TypeScript service teams.
tradeoffs: More ceremony than minimal Node web stacks.
The framework can be overkill for small utilities or very simple APIs.
best_for: TypeScript service platforms with multiple modules and explicit dependency boundaries.
Teams that want Angular-like application structure on the backend.
avoid_when: A simple route layer and thin service set are sufficient.
The team is optimizing for minimum abstraction rather than long-lived structure.

stacks.name: PostgreSQL
strengths: Strong relational model, transactional guarantees, mature indexing, and extension ecosystem.
Usually the right default system of record for business applications.
Can host traditional relational data and selected retrieval features in one operational system.
tradeoffs: Not every workload belongs in one primary database instance.
Teams often underinvest in schema, indexing, and maintenance discipline when Postgres is treated as a generic bucket.
best_for: Canonical business data, workflow state, audit trails, and relational joins.
Applications that need strong consistency and long-lived schema ownership.
avoid_when: The team is trying to use the primary OLTP store as a substitute for every queue, analytics, and blob workload.
Schema ownership is unclear or migrations are unmanaged.

stacks.name: SQLAlchemy
strengths: Flexible Python data layer that supports both ORM and explicit SQL construction.
Good for teams that need precision around relational behavior without abandoning Python models.
Strong fit with FastAPI and service-oriented Python stacks.
tradeoffs: More design freedom means more room for inconsistent session and loading patterns.
Teams need discipline around transactions, lazy loading, and query shape.
best_for: Python services with relational models that still need explicit query control.
Applications where the team wants to tune joins and loading strategies rather than hide them.
avoid_when: The team wants a highly declarative single-schema workflow like Prisma in a TypeScript stack.
Developers are likely to treat ORM defaults as performance-safe without review.

stacks.name: Prisma
strengths: Type-safe client and schema-first workflow give TypeScript teams a clear data ownership model.
Migrations, generated client, and schema definitions work well as one toolkit.
Good fit for application teams that prioritize delivery speed and compile-time safety.
tradeoffs: Less appropriate when the team needs unrestricted SQL as the default path.
Query abstractions can hide inefficient access patterns if data shape is not reviewed.
best_for: TypeScript backends and full-stack apps that want schema, migration, and query tooling aligned.
Teams that benefit from generated clients and strong IDE support.
avoid_when: Raw SQL is the dominant integration surface.
The data platform is Python-first or heavily driven by non-Node tooling.

stacks.name: Redis
strengths: Extremely effective for low-latency caching, transient coordination, and queue-adjacent workloads.
Useful as a broker and fast data structure engine near the request path.
Can support vector and AI-oriented patterns when low-latency memory-first access matters.
tradeoffs: Easy to misuse as a primary system of record.
Durability and operational semantics depend heavily on deployment mode and use case.
best_for: Caching, tokens, rate limits, broker roles, transient coordination, and short-lived state.
Latency-sensitive derived data rather than canonical business truth.
avoid_when: The data requires strong relational queries and durable auditability as the default.
Teams are tempted to keep business-critical state only in cache-shaped storage.

stacks.name: Celery
strengths: Good fit for conventional background job processing in Python stacks.
Supports retries, scheduling, task routing, and horizontal worker scaling.
Integrates naturally with Django and other Python web frameworks.
tradeoffs: Simple jobs are easy; complex long-running business workflows can become hard to reason about.
Broker and result backend choices materially affect reliability and operability.
best_for: Email, exports, ingest jobs, image processing, periodic tasks, and async work off the request path.
Python products that need standard queue-driven workers rather than workflow orchestration.
avoid_when: Workflows need durable step-by-step state, human waits, or years-long execution.
The team is modeling long business processes as opaque chains of ad hoc tasks.

stacks.name: Temporal
strengths: Durable workflow execution with replay, persisted event history, and clear execution state.
Better fit than task queues for long-running business processes with retries, timers, signals, and child workflows.
Makes workflow state an explicit platform concern instead of hidden glue code.
tradeoffs: More infrastructure and conceptual overhead than simple queue workers.
Requires deterministic workflow design and explicit modeling discipline.
best_for: Provisioning flows, approvals, recovery logic, multi-step integrations, and durable cross-system business processes.
Any workflow where waiting, retries, and visibility are first-class requirements.
avoid_when: The work is just a short fire-and-forget background task.
The team is not prepared to treat workflow design as application architecture.

stacks.name: Docker
strengths: Makes the application package a portable test and deploy unit.
Improves parity across local, CI, and production environments.
Useful even before full orchestration is justified.
tradeoffs: Containerization does not solve deployment topology or observability by itself.
Poor image hygiene can simply encapsulate bad environments instead of fixing them.
best_for: Standardizing app packaging and dependency isolation.
CI/CD pipelines and teams that need reproducible runtime environments.
avoid_when: The team expects Docker alone to replace platform design, secrets strategy, or runtime operations.
A simple script or local-only utility does not need image lifecycle overhead.

stacks.name: Kubernetes
strengths: Provides resilient deployment, scaling, load balancing, and self-healing for containerized workloads.
Strong fit once multiple services, environments, or operational controls must be standardized.
Acts as a platform substrate rather than an app framework.
tradeoffs: Operationally expensive for small products that do not need platform-level control.
It is not a PaaS and does not remove the need for logging, monitoring, CI/CD, or service design choices.
best_for: Multi-service platforms, workload isolation, controlled rollouts, and autoscaling environments.
Organizations that need a durable internal platform rather than ad hoc host management.
avoid_when: A single app or small product can be operated more simply on managed app hosting.
The team lacks the operational maturity to own cluster-level complexity.

stacks.name: OpenTelemetry
strengths: Standardizes telemetry generation across services and runtimes.
Reduces vendor lock-in by separating instrumentation from backend choice.
Critical for debugging distributed systems and workflow-heavy platforms.
tradeoffs: Instrumentation without conventions creates noisy telemetry rather than observability.
The tooling is not itself a backend, so teams still need storage and visualization decisions.
best_for: Cross-service tracing, request correlation, and telemetry normalization.
Any platform that expects multiple components, queues, and workflows to interact.
avoid_when: The team is not ready to define meaningful spans, attributes, and service boundaries.
There is no downstream backend or collector plan.

stacks.name: Playwright
strengths: Browser automation with strong isolation, modern engine support, and CI suitability.
Useful for user-path verification, UI contract checks, and visual regression capture.
Fits both explicit end-to-end tests and targeted smoke/golden workflows.
tradeoffs: End-to-end tests become brittle if they replace lower-level contract or unit coverage.
Golden tests need normalization discipline to avoid false diffs.
best_for: Critical browser flows, route-level regressions, and screenshot baselines.
Verifying integration behavior that unit tests cannot prove.
avoid_when: The team is trying to use browser tests as the first line of debugging for all logic.
UI volatility is high and selectors/contracts are unmanaged.

stacks.name: pgvector
strengths: Keeps vectors close to relational data, joins, and transactional controls.
Supports exact and approximate nearest-neighbor search plus hybrid search with full-text search.
Useful when retrieval should remain inside the main Postgres operating model.
tradeoffs: Approximate indexes require recall tuning and operational review.
Vector search alone is not enough for authoritative or policy-driven repo understanding.
best_for: Knowledge systems that need semantic search layered on top of canonical relational metadata.
Applications that want hybrid lexical plus vector retrieval in PostgreSQL.
avoid_when: Teams expect embeddings to replace structured facts, ownership rules, or explicit relations.
Operational scale clearly exceeds what the current Postgres footprint should carry.

stacks.name: LangChain Retrieval Patterns
strengths: Makes retrieval pipelines explicit: ingestion, chunking, embeddings, retrieval, and answer generation.
Useful vocabulary for separating simple two-step RAG from agentic and validated hybrid RAG.
Reinforces that an existing SQL or document system can remain authoritative while retrieval layers sit on top.
tradeoffs: Agentic retrieval can add latency and unpredictability when simpler two-step retrieval would work.
Naive chunk-and-embed pipelines often ignore authority, freshness, and structured metadata.
best_for: Knowledge systems that need explicit retrieval architecture choices.
Teams evaluating when to keep retrieval deterministic versus agentic.
avoid_when: The team is trying to bolt semantic search onto a domain that lacks curated source-of-truth structure.
Complex agentic retrieval is being introduced before deterministic retrieval quality is proven.

stacks.name: OpenAI API
strengths: Mature API with chat completions, embeddings, function calling, and structured outputs.
text-embedding-3-small (1536d) and text-embedding-3-large (3072d) with dimension reduction via dimensions param.
Function calling with strict mode enforces JSON Schema conformance; all fields required, additionalProperties: false.
Tool search defers rarely-used tools and loads them on demand, reducing token cost.
Namespaces group related tools by domain (crm, billing) for better routing in large tool surfaces.
Structured Outputs via response_format guarantees schema adherence using Pydantic/zod integration.
Custom tools with context-free grammars (Lark/regex) constrain free-form tool outputs.
Prompt caching, streaming, batch API, and compaction support production cost optimization.
tradeoffs: Vendor-locked to OpenAI infrastructure and pricing model.
Function calling adds token overhead; strict mode increases first-call latency from schema processing.
Structured Outputs limited to 5000 properties, 10 nesting levels; some JSON Schema features unsupported.
best_for: LLM-powered applications needing embeddings, chat, and tool use.
Production systems requiring prompt engineering, eval pipelines, and RAG integration.
Agent systems needing reliable structured output with schema enforcement.
avoid_when: Latency or cost constraints require on-premise or open-weight models.
The application can run with simpler deterministic logic instead of LLM inference.
best_practices: Use strict: true on all function definitions for reliable schema conformance.
Keep initially available functions under 20 per turn; use tool search for large tool surfaces.
Use namespaces to group tools by domain (crm.get_customer, billing.create_invoice).
Write clear function descriptions and parameter documentation; pass the 'intern test'.
Offload burden from the model: don't make it fill arguments you already know.
Combine functions that are always called in sequence into a single function.
Use Pydantic models with responses.parse() for type-safe structured output handling.
Handle refusals: check response.output_parsed for None and response.refusal for safety-based refusals.
For parallel tool calls, handle all function_call items in response.output; pass back reasoning items for reasoning models.
Use tool_choice: 'required' when at least one tool call is always expected; 'auto' for flexible behavior.

stacks.name: Anthropic Claude API
strengths: Client tools (user-executed) and server tools (web_search, code_execution, web_fetch, tool_search) provide flexible agent patterns.
Strict tool use mode (strict: true) forces the model to always match tool schema exactly.
Server tools run on Anthropic infrastructure; client tools return tool_use blocks for your code to execute.
Agentic loop pattern: request -> tool_use (stop_reason) -> execute -> tool_result -> continue until done.
Strong reasoning capabilities; benchmark gains (LAB-Bench, SWE-bench) with even basic tool access.
tradeoffs: Server tools add per-request token overhead (~346 tokens for auto/none, ~313 for any/tool in Claude 4+ models).
Tool definitions injected as system prompt tokens; monitor tool_use input token cost.
Fewer built-in structured output modes compared to OpenAI's strict function calling.
best_for: Agentic systems needing tool orchestration and complex reasoning chains.
Applications requiring built-in web search or code execution as server-side tools.
Multi-step workflows with tool_use -> tool_result conversation loops.
avoid_when: The application only needs simple text generation without tool use.
Cost sensitivity requires the cheapest possible per-token pricing.
best_practices: Use strict: true on tool definitions to guarantee schema conformance.
Handle the agentic loop: check stop_reason for 'tool_use', execute tool, return tool_result, repeat.
Use server tools (web_search, code_execution) for tasks that benefit from Anthropic-infrastructure execution.
Use MCP connector for standardized tool servers across multiple LLM hosts.
Monitor tool token overhead: ~313-346 additional system prompt tokens per request with tools.

stacks.name: Model Context Protocol
strengths: Standardized host/client/server architecture for LLM tool integration via JSON-RPC 2.0.
Three primitives: Tools (model-controlled actions), Resources (application-controlled data), Prompts (user-controlled templates).
Transport layer supports stdio (local) and Streamable HTTP (remote); lifecycle management with capability negotiation.
tradeoffs: Protocol is evolving; breaking changes may occur in transport and capability specs.
Adds architectural complexity compared to direct function calling in a single LLM API.
best_for: Building reusable tool servers that work across multiple LLM hosts and editors.
Multi-agent systems where standardized tool discovery and execution matters.
avoid_when: Simple single-LLM applications where direct function calling suffices.
The team cannot maintain MCP server infrastructure alongside the main application.

stacks.name: ChromaDB
strengths: Open-source, lightweight vector DB with automatic embedding via built-in models.
Collections API supports add, query, upsert, and delete with metadata filtering.
In-memory client for prototyping, persistent client for local apps, cloud client for production.
tradeoffs: Fewer advanced index tuning options compared to Weaviate or Pinecone.
Cloud offering is newer and less battle-tested at extreme scale.
best_for: Rapid semantic search prototyping and lightweight RAG pipelines.
Python-first applications that need a quick embedded vector store.
avoid_when: Production workloads require managed infrastructure with SLAs and high availability.
Advanced index types (HNSW tuning, quantization) are critical performance requirements.

stacks.name: Weaviate
strengths: Multiple vector index types: HNSW (default, logarithmic search), flat (small/multi-tenant), dynamic (auto-switches flat-to-HNSW), HFresh (cluster-based, memory-efficient).
Supports keyword (BM25F), vector, and hybrid search with reranking and integrated RAG.
Named vectors allow multiple vector spaces per collection; async indexing for high-throughput imports.
tradeoffs: Operationally heavier than lightweight embedded vector stores like ChromaDB.
HNSW index requires all vectors in memory for optimal performance; can be memory-intensive at scale.
best_for: Production semantic search needing hybrid keyword+vector retrieval with reranking.
Multi-tenant SaaS with per-tenant isolated datasets using flat or dynamic indexes.
avoid_when: A simple in-memory vector store suffices for prototyping.
The team cannot manage vector DB infrastructure alongside the primary data store.

stacks.name: LlamaIndex
strengths: Five-stage RAG architecture: Loading (connectors/readers), Indexing (documents/nodes), Storing (vector/index persistence), Querying (retrievers/routers/postprocessors/synthesizers), Evaluation.
Retriever abstraction supports vector, keyword, hybrid, and custom retrieval strategies.
Router and node postprocessor patterns enable multi-index queries and result refinement.
tradeoffs: Abstractions can hide retrieval quality issues if not tuned carefully.
Framework coupling makes it harder to swap individual retrieval components.
best_for: Structured RAG pipelines with explicit ingestion, indexing, and query stages.
Applications needing multi-source retrieval with routing between different indexes.
avoid_when: Simple direct embedding lookup suffices without pipeline orchestration.
The team prefers lower-level control over each retrieval step.

stacks.name: Pydantic
strengths: Type-hint driven validation with BaseModel, TypeAdapter, and model_validate/model_validate_json.
Field validators (before/after/wrap/plain), model validators, and custom error types for complex constraints.
Generic models, RootModel, frozen immutability, ConfigDict for extra/strict/from_attributes behavior.
JSON Schema generation via model_json_schema(); tight FastAPI integration for API contracts.
tradeoffs: V2 migration from V1 requires updating method names and config patterns.
Validator ordering (right-to-left for before/wrap, left-to-right for after) requires care.
best_for: API request/response validation, configuration management, and data pipeline contracts.
Any Python system where runtime type safety and serialization matter.
avoid_when: Simple dataclasses or TypedDict suffice without validation logic.
Performance-critical hot paths where dataclass construction is measurably faster.

stacks.name: pytest
strengths: Fixture system with scopes (function/class/module/package/session), yield teardown, autouse, and factory patterns.
Parametrize decorator for data-driven tests; pytest.param with marks for conditional execution.
conftest.py for fixture sharing across modules; fixtures can request other fixtures for composability.
Markers, custom hooks (pytest_generate_tests), and introspection via request object.
Dynamic fixture scope via callable; fixture overriding at folder/module/test level.
tradeoffs: Advanced fixture dependency graphs can become hard to trace without clear naming conventions.
Parametrized tests with large datasets need explicit IDs to maintain readable output.
Yield fixture teardown runs in reverse order; finalizer ordering requires care.
best_for: Python test suites needing structured fixtures, parametrization, and plugin extensibility.
Projects requiring test coverage, async testing, or property-based testing integration.
avoid_when: The project is not Python-based.
Simple assertion scripts suffice without fixture or parametrization needs.
best_practices: Use yield fixtures for teardown; place cleanup code after yield for automatic execution on test completion.
Keep fixtures atomic: each fixture should make one state-changing action with its own teardown.
Use scope='session' for expensive resources (DB connections, Docker containers); scope='function' for test isolation.
Use factory-as-fixture pattern when a test needs multiple instances from the same fixture.
Use autouse=True sparingly; prefer explicit fixture requests for clarity.
Use request.node.get_closest_marker() to pass data from markers into fixtures.
Use conftest.py at different directory levels for scope-appropriate fixture overriding.
Use pytest.param(value, marks=pytest.mark.skip) to conditionally skip specific parametrize values.
Use dynamic scope (callable scope) when fixture lifetime depends on CLI flags (e.g., --keep-containers).
Use @pytest.mark.usefixtures for class-level fixtures that don't need direct reference in test methods.
Combine Hypothesis @given with pytest fixtures and parametrize for layered coverage.
Use addfinalizer for safe teardown when multiple cleanup steps must execute independently.

stacks.name: Ruff
strengths: Extremely fast Rust-based Python linter and formatter replacing flake8, isort, black, and pycodestyle.
Hierarchical config via pyproject.toml/ruff.toml with per-file-ignores and extend patterns.
Default rules include Pyflakes (F) and pycodestyle subset (E4/E7/E9); supports 800+ rules from 50+ plugins.
Auto-fix with --fix, preview mode for unstable rules, and Jupyter notebook support.
tradeoffs: Not all flake8 plugins have ruff equivalents yet.
Rapid release cadence means rule behavior can change between versions.
best_for: Python projects needing fast, unified linting and formatting in CI and editor.
Replacing multiple legacy tools (flake8, isort, black) with one fast binary.
avoid_when: The project requires a specific flake8 plugin with no ruff equivalent.
The team prefers mypy-only type checking without linting concerns.

stacks.name: GitHub Actions
strengths: Workflow YAML in .github/workflows with event triggers (push, pull_request, schedule, workflow_dispatch).
Jobs run on GitHub-hosted or self-hosted runners with matrix strategies for multi-platform testing.
Security hardening: secrets in env vars (not interpolated in scripts), OIDC for cloud auth, Dependabot for action updates.
tradeoffs: Complex workflows with many jobs can become hard to debug without structured logging.
Self-hosted runners require security hardening (JIT runners, restricted network, no persistent state).
best_for: GitHub-hosted projects needing CI/CD, automated testing, and deployment pipelines.
Open source projects leveraging free GitHub-hosted runner minutes.
avoid_when: The project requires complex multi-repo orchestration better suited to dedicated CI platforms.
Self-hosted runner security requirements exceed the team's operational capacity.

stacks.name: OWASP API Security
strengths: OWASP API Security Top 10 (2023): Broken Object Level Authorization, Broken Authentication, Broken Object Property Level Authorization, Unrestricted Resource Consumption, Broken Function Level Authorization.
Also covers: Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Business Flows, SSRF, Security Misconfiguration, Improper Inventory Management, Unsafe Consumption of APIs.
Provides actionable prevention checklists for each risk category.
tradeoffs: Checklist-oriented; teams must still design security architecture specific to their domain.
Does not replace runtime security tooling (WAF, SAST, DAST).
best_for: API security threat modeling and design reviews.
Compliance baselines and security audit preparation.
avoid_when: The team treats the checklist as a substitute for security engineering culture.
The application has no API surface exposed to external consumers.

stacks.name: Python Asyncio
strengths: TaskGroup (3.11+) provides structured concurrency: if any task fails, siblings are cancelled and all exceptions propagate as ExceptionGroup.
asyncio.timeout context manager (3.11+) for scoped deadline enforcement; replaces wait_for for clarity.
asyncio.to_thread offloads blocking I/O to thread pool without losing async flow control.
Eager task factory (3.12+) starts coroutines synchronously up to first await for reduced scheduling overhead.
Runner context manager (3.11+) provides reusable event loop for REPL and testing without asyncio.run per call.
tradeoffs: create_task returns weak references; tasks must be saved to a set to prevent garbage collection of running tasks.
CancelledError is a BaseException (3.9+), not Exception; bare except clauses can silently swallow cancellation.
Mixing sync and async code requires explicit to_thread/run_coroutine_threadsafe bridges.
TaskGroup does not support fire-and-forget patterns; all tasks must complete or cancel.
best_for: High-concurrency I/O-bound services: HTTP servers, database clients, file processing pipelines.
Structured concurrent workflows where error propagation and cleanup must be explicit.
avoid_when: CPU-bound workloads that need true parallelism (use multiprocessing or ProcessPoolExecutor).
Simple scripts where threading or synchronous I/O are sufficient.
best_practices: Use TaskGroup instead of gather for new code: explicit error handling, automatic cancellation on failure.
Always save task references: background_tasks = set(); task = asyncio.create_task(coro()); background_tasks.add(task); task.add_done_callback(background_tasks.discard).
Prefer asyncio.timeout over asyncio.wait_for for scoped deadline management.
Use asyncio.to_thread for blocking library calls (file I/O, legacy sync APIs).
Never call asyncio.run inside an already-running event loop; use Runner or nest_asyncio for testing.
Handle CancelledError explicitly in cleanup paths; use task.uncancel() only when resuming cancelled work intentionally.

stacks.name: Python Typing
strengths: Protocol classes enable structural subtyping (duck typing with static checking) without inheritance.
TypeVar with bounds/constraints enables generic functions and classes with type safety.
ParamSpec (3.10+) preserves function signatures through decorators for accurate type checking.
TypeGuard and TypeIs (3.10+/3.12+) enable user-defined type narrowing in conditionals.
Generic syntax [T] (3.12+) and type statement simplify generic class/function/alias definitions.
tradeoffs: Runtime overhead is zero for annotations but Protocol/TypeVar require understanding of variance.
TypeGuard narrows only the argument type, not the containing scope; TypeIs narrows both but has stricter requirements.
NewType creates a distinct type for static checking but is identity function at runtime.
best_for: Library and framework code where API contracts must be precise and self-documenting.
Teams using mypy, pyright, or similar type checkers for CI-level type safety.
Plugin architectures using Protocol for structural interfaces.
avoid_when: Quick scripts or prototypes where type annotation overhead exceeds benefit.
The team has no type checker configured and annotations would be unchecked documentation.
best_practices: Use Protocol for structural subtyping instead of ABCs when you only need method signature matching.
Define TypeVar with bound=SomeBase to constrain generic types to a class hierarchy.
Use ParamSpec in decorator types to preserve the wrapped function's signature for IDE support.
Prefer TypeIs over TypeGuard (3.12+) for type narrowing that works in both positive and negative branches.
Use NewType for domain-specific primitives (UserId, EmailAddress) to prevent accidental mixing.
Use TypedDict for dictionary shapes with known keys; use total=False for optional keys.
Annotate with collections.abc types (Sequence, Mapping) rather than concrete types (list, dict) for parameters.

stacks.name: Python Dataclasses
strengths: frozen=True creates immutable instances with __hash__ support, ideal for value objects and dict keys.
slots=True (3.10+) generates __slots__ for reduced memory footprint and faster attribute access.
kw_only=True (3.10+) forces keyword-only constructor arguments for clarity in large dataclasses.
__post_init__ enables derived field computation and validation after construction.
field(default_factory=list) prevents the mutable default argument pitfall.
tradeoffs: No built-in validation; use Pydantic when runtime type checking and coercion are needed.
Inheritance ordering follows MRO; fields from parent classes appear first in __init__.
match_args=True (default 3.10+) enables structural pattern matching but adds generated __match_args__.
best_for: Internal data containers, DTOs, and configuration objects without complex validation.
Value objects in domain-driven design where immutability and equality semantics matter.
avoid_when: Input data needs runtime validation and coercion (use Pydantic instead).
The class needs extensive custom __init__ logic beyond what __post_init__ provides.
best_practices: Use frozen=True for value objects that should be hashable and immutable.
Use slots=True for memory-intensive applications with many instances.
Always use field(default_factory=list) for mutable defaults, never a bare mutable literal.
Use kw_only=True or KW_ONLY sentinel for dataclasses with many fields to prevent positional argument mistakes.
Use InitVar for constructor-only parameters that should not be stored as fields.
Prefer dataclasses over NamedTuple when you need mutability or complex field defaults.

stacks.name: Python Logging
strengths: Hierarchical logger namespace via getLogger(__name__) automatically follows module structure.
Handler/Formatter/Filter separation enables routing logs to multiple destinations with different formats.
LoggerAdapter adds contextual fields (request_id, user_id) without modifying logger calls.
Thread-safe by design; handlers use internal locks for concurrent access.
captureWarnings(True) routes Python warnings through the logging system for unified output.
tradeoffs: basicConfig convenience function only works on first call; subsequent calls are no-ops unless force=True.
Root logger propagation can cause duplicate messages if handlers are added at multiple levels.
No built-in structured (JSON) logging; requires custom formatter or third-party library (structlog, python-json-logger).
best_for: Application and library logging with hierarchical namespace control.
Audit trails, request tracing, and structured error reporting.
avoid_when: Simple print-based debugging suffices for a throwaway script.
The application needs high-throughput structured event streaming (consider dedicated event systems).
best_practices: Always use logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) in libraries; never configure the root logger from library code.
Set propagate=False on loggers with their own handlers to prevent duplicate messages.
Use LoggerAdapter or logging.Filter to inject request-scoped context (request_id, trace_id).
Use logging.config.dictConfig for application-level configuration; avoid basicConfig in production code.
Log at appropriate levels: DEBUG for development diagnostics, INFO for operational events, WARNING for recoverable issues, ERROR for failures, CRITICAL for system-level failures.
Include taskName (3.12+) in formatters for asyncio task identification in logs.
Never log sensitive data (passwords, tokens, PII) even at DEBUG level.

stacks.name: Hypothesis
strengths: @given decorator with strategies generates hundreds of random inputs per test, finding edge cases manual tests miss.
Automatic shrinking: when a failing case is found, Hypothesis minimizes it to the simplest reproducing example.
@composite strategy lets you build dependent generators where one drawn value constrains the next.
Works seamlessly with pytest fixtures, markers, and parametrize decorators.
Database persistence: failing examples are saved and replayed on future runs for regression catching.
tradeoffs: Tests run slower than unit tests due to many generated inputs (default 100 examples per test).
Requires thinking in terms of properties and invariants rather than specific input/output pairs.
assume() and filter() can discard many generated values, reducing effective coverage if overused.
best_for: Testing pure functions, serialization round-trips, and mathematical invariants.
Finding edge cases in parsers, validators, data transformations, and encoding/decoding logic.
Complementing example-based tests with generative property-based coverage.
avoid_when: The function under test has complex side effects that are expensive to set up and tear down.
Properties are difficult to express (e.g., the only specification is 'it matches the expected output').
best_practices: Start with simple strategies (st.integers, st.text, st.lists) and compose with .map() and .filter().
Use @composite for dependent generation where one value constrains subsequent draws.
Prefer .filter() on strategies over assume() in test body for clearer intent and less waste.
Combine @given with @pytest.mark.parametrize for orthogonal coverage dimensions.
Use st.data() for interactive drawing inside the test body when generation depends on computed state.
Set max_examples via settings profile for CI (more examples) vs local development (fewer examples).
Use hypothesis[cli] to manage the example database and reproduction across CI runs.

stacks.name: uv
strengths: 10-100x faster than pip for dependency resolution and installation, written in Rust.
Unified tool: replaces pip, pip-tools, virtualenv, pyenv, and pipx in a single binary.
Universal lockfile (uv.lock) ensures deterministic cross-platform dependency resolution.
Built-in Python version management: uv python install and automatic version discovery.
uv run command auto-creates virtualenvs and runs scripts with proper dependency isolation.
tradeoffs: Newer tool with less community documentation than pip/pipenv/poetry.
Some edge cases with legacy packages that rely on pip-specific installation behavior.
Lockfile format is uv-specific; not directly compatible with pip-tools requirements.txt.
best_for: New Python projects wanting fast, modern dependency management with lockfiles.
CI/CD pipelines where installation speed directly impacts build times.
Monorepos and workspaces needing shared dependency resolution.
avoid_when: The project is deeply integrated with poetry or pipenv and migration cost is high.
Legacy deployment systems require pip freeze / requirements.txt as the only supported format.
best_practices: Use uv init to scaffold new projects with pyproject.toml and uv.lock.
Run uv sync to install exact locked dependencies; use uv lock to update the lockfile.
Use uv run <command> instead of activating virtualenvs manually.
Define optional dependency groups in pyproject.toml for dev/test/docs extras.
Pin Python version with .python-version file for team consistency.
Use uv tool install for global CLI tools (ruff, mkdocs) without polluting project environments.

stacks.name: Prompt Engineering
strengths: Output contracts (format, length, section order) reduce drift and improve token efficiency.
Verification loops (correctness, grounding, formatting checks) catch errors before high-impact actions.
Completeness contracts with internal checklists and blocked-item marking prevent partial execution.
Tool persistence rules with dependency checks ensure prerequisite steps are not skipped.
Research mode (plan-retrieve-synthesize 3-pass) improves multi-source synthesis quality.
tradeoffs: More explicit prompts increase input token cost; balance verbosity against accuracy gains.
Prompt patterns that work for large models may over-constrain small models (nano/mini).
Reasoning effort is not one-size-fits-all; higher effort increases latency without guaranteed quality gains.
best_for: Agent systems requiring reliable tool use, structured output, and multi-step completion.
Production assistants needing consistent formatting, citation accuracy, and safety compliance.
Research and synthesis workflows needing disciplined evidence gathering and verification.
avoid_when: Simple text generation where default model behavior is sufficient.
The application can enforce output structure through structured outputs (JSON Schema) instead.
best_practices: Start with the smallest prompt that passes your evals; add blocks only when they fix a measured failure mode.
Use XML-like tags (<output_contract>, <verification_loop>, <tool_persistence_rules>) for clear section boundaries.
Define instruction priority explicitly: user instructions override defaults; safety constraints never yield.
For mid-conversation updates, use scoped <task_update> blocks that state scope, override, and carry-forward.
Default reasoning effort: none/low for execution-heavy; medium/high for research-heavy; xhigh only if evals prove benefit.
Add <empty_result_recovery> to handle partial or empty retrieval results with fallback strategies.
Separate persistent personality from per-response writing controls for customer-facing workflows.
Pin citation rules: only cite retrieved sources, never fabricate, attach to specific claims.

stacks.name: OWASP Authentication & REST Security
strengths: Layered authentication: password policy (min 8 w/MFA, max 64+, block breached passwords), MFA (stops 99.9% of compromises), adaptive/risk-based auth.
JWT validation discipline: verify issuer, audience, expiry, not-before, and algorithm; reject alg:none; prefer asymmetric signing.
REST hardening: restrict HTTP methods (405), validate Content-Type (406/415), enforce CORS, apply Cache-Control/HSTS/X-Content-Type-Options headers.
State machine patterns for out-of-order API execution prevention in multi-step workflows.
tradeoffs: Comprehensive checklists require implementation effort proportional to the threat surface.
Generic error messages (prevent user enumeration) can frustrate UX; balance security with usability.
best_for: APIs exposed to external consumers or mobile clients needing authentication hardening.
Multi-step workflows requiring CSRF protection, session management, and reauthentication for sensitive operations.
avoid_when: Internal-only services behind strong network controls where simplified auth suffices.
The application has no user-facing authentication surface.
best_practices: Store passwords with Argon2id or bcrypt; never use MD5/SHA1 for password hashing.
Implement exponential backoff for failed login attempts; consider account lockout after threshold.
Use constant-time comparison for credential verification to prevent timing attacks.
Return generic error messages for auth failures: 'Login failed; Invalid user ID or password' prevents enumeration.
Validate JWT: check iss, aud, exp, nbf, and algorithm; reject tokens with alg:none.
Apply rate limiting with 429 status codes; use API keys for identification, not authentication.
Set security headers: Strict-Transport-Security, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff, X-Frame-Options, Content-Security-Policy.
Require reauthentication for sensitive operations (password change, payment, admin actions).

stacks.name: LangGraph
strengths: StateGraph model with typed nodes, edges, and conditional routing enables complex agent topologies.
Durable execution: agents persist through failures and resume from exactly where they left off.
Human-in-the-loop via interrupt_before/interrupt_after lets humans inspect and modify state mid-execution.
MemorySaver for in-memory checkpointing; PostgreSQL and Redis backends for persistent cross-session state.
Subgraphs enable composable multi-agent systems with isolated state and independent lifecycle.
Streaming output at token, state, event, and message levels for responsive agent UX.
tradeoffs: Low-level API requires more explicit state management than high-level agent frameworks.
Debugging complex graph topologies requires LangSmith or custom observability tooling.
Learning curve for StateGraph design patterns when coming from simple chain-based architecture.
best_for: Complex multi-step agents needing durable state and failure recovery.
Stateful conversational flows requiring persistent memory across sessions.
Human-oversight workflows where agent state must be inspectable and modifiable.
Multi-agent architectures where independent subgraphs collaborate on a shared task.
avoid_when: Simple single-turn LLM calls or basic retrieval pipelines that do not need stateful loops.
The team cannot maintain graph topology complexity without dedicated observability tooling.
best_practices: Define state as a TypedDict or Pydantic model; use reducers for message accumulation.
Use MemorySaver during development; switch to PostgreSQL/Redis checkpointer for production persistence.
Use interrupt_before/interrupt_after to add human review gates without redesigning graph topology.
Decompose complex agents into subgraphs with clear input/output state boundaries.
Use streaming events to give users real-time feedback during long-running agent loops.
Pair with LangSmith for graph visualization, state transition tracing, and eval pipelines.

stacks.name: Instructor
strengths: Pydantic-based structured output extraction from 15+ LLM providers with a single unified API.
instructor.from_provider('openai/gpt-4o') pattern works across OpenAI, Anthropic, Gemini, and others.
Automatic validation retry with max_retries: invalid LLM outputs are re-requested until valid.
Streaming partial and iterable support for progressive extraction of structured objects.
Jinja templating for prompt composition and llm_validator for semantic field validation via LLM.
Hooks system for logging, monitoring, and tracing extraction calls.
3M+ monthly downloads; widely adopted for production LLM data pipelines.
tradeoffs: Adds a library dependency around LLM API calls; debugging retry loops requires understanding the retry strategy.
Does not replace a full LLM gateway like LiteLLM; focused specifically on structured output extraction.
best_for: Extracting structured data from unstructured text or LLM responses.
Applications needing Pydantic-validated LLM output with automatic retry on schema violations.
Multi-provider LLM applications where structured output format consistency is required.
avoid_when: The application only needs raw LLM text output without structured parsing.
A full LLM gateway with cost tracking and provider routing is needed (use LiteLLM instead or alongside).
best_practices: Always define Pydantic response models with field descriptions; descriptions guide LLM output quality.
Use max_retries to handle transient schema violations; set a reasonable budget (2-3 retries).
Use create_with_completion to access raw API response alongside parsed model for debugging.
Use streaming iterables for list extraction to reduce time-to-first-result.
Add field-level validators (Pydantic @field_validator) for semantic constraints beyond JSON Schema.

stacks.name: LiteLLM
strengths: Single completion() interface for 100+ LLM providers using OpenAI format: no per-provider SDK switching.
Router with automatic retry, fallback, and load-balancing across multiple model deployments.
Proxy Server (LLM Gateway): self-hosted OpenAI-compatible endpoint for all backend providers.
Virtual keys with per-key, per-team, and per-user spend budgets and rate limits.
Built-in cost tracking per response; supports Langfuse, MLflow, Helicone, and OpenTelemetry callbacks.
MCP gateway support and A2A agent protocol compatibility.
Consistent exception types across all providers for uniform error handling.
tradeoffs: Proxy server adds infrastructure complexity; requires careful virtual key and budget management.
Provider abstraction may hide provider-specific features not exposed in the unified interface.
Router configuration for complex fallback trees requires careful upfront planning.
best_for: Multi-provider LLM applications needing a single interface with fallback and cost control.
Teams running a shared LLM gateway for cost visibility, virtual key management, and provider flexibility.
Production agent systems requiring retry logic, load balancing, and observability across model calls.
avoid_when: Single-provider applications where direct SDK offers sufficient control.
The team cannot maintain a proxy server deployment alongside the main application.
best_practices: Use litellm.Router for production: configure multiple deployments per model with retry and fallback policies.
Set up virtual keys in the Proxy Server for team/service isolation and budget enforcement.
Use success_callback and failure_callback for observability; hook into Langfuse or OpenTelemetry for tracing.
Use consistent model string format: 'provider/model-name' (e.g., 'anthropic/claude-3-5-sonnet-20241022').
Test fallback chains with mock failures before production; verify budget limits in staging.

stacks.name: Google Gemini API
strengths: Gemini 2.5 Flash (frontier-class, cost-efficient) and Pro (best multimodal reasoning) models.
Million-token long context window enables full codebase, document corpus, or video ingestion in one request.
Native function calling and structured outputs via JSON Schema for schema-constrained generation.
Built-in tools: Google Search, URL Context, Code Execution, Maps, and computer use capabilities.
Live API for real-time low-latency voice and audio streaming with interruption handling.
Thinking mode for multi-step complex reasoning with transparent chain-of-thought.
OpenAI-compatible endpoint: existing openai library code works with a base_url swap.
google.genai Python SDK with async support and streaming.
tradeoffs: Google infrastructure dependency; data residency and compliance requirements need careful review.
Some advanced tool capabilities (computer use) are in early access with evolving APIs.
Pricing model differs from OpenAI; long-context requests need cost modeling before production rollout.
best_for: Applications needing very long context windows (millions of tokens) without chunking.
Multimodal tasks spanning text, vision, audio, and video in a single API.
Google ecosystem integrations where Search, Maps, or URL grounding add direct value.
avoid_when: Strict data residency requirements that conflict with Google cloud regions.
The application is tightly coupled to OpenAI-specific features not covered by the compatibility layer.
best_practices: Use the OpenAI-compatible endpoint for easy migration from existing OpenAI-based code.
Use structured outputs with JSON Schema for schema-constrained responses; avoids prompt-only formatting.
Leverage Google Search tool for grounded, citation-backed responses to factual queries.
Use URL Context tool to ground responses on specific web pages without manual fetch-and-paste.
Test long-context requests with representative payload sizes before committing to the context strategy.
Use thinking mode for complex multi-step reasoning; disable for simple tasks to reduce cost and latency.

stacks.name: Hugging Face Transformers
strengths: Framework for 1M+ pretrained model checkpoints on Hugging Face Hub covering text, vision, audio, video, and multimodal tasks.
Pipeline class for simple single-line inference: text-generation, ASR, image-segmentation, zero-shot-classification, and more.
Trainer class for fine-tuning with mixed precision, torch.compile, FlashAttention, and distributed training (FSDP, DeepSpeed).
generate() method for LLM text generation with sampling, beam search, greedy decode, and streaming.
Pivot to any inference backend: vLLM, SGLang, TGI (Text Generation Inference), Axolotl, Unsloth.
3-class architecture: PretrainedConfig (model params), PreTrainedModel (forward pass), Preprocessor (tokenizers/processors).
Strong community: model cards, datasets, spaces, and Hugging Face Hub integration.
tradeoffs: Full training stack adds significant dependency weight; use Pipeline for inference-only use cases.
GPU memory management requires attention; large models need quantization or multi-GPU setup.
Model license diversity on Hub requires per-model license review before production deployment.
best_for: Teams loading and fine-tuning open-weight models for specialized domain tasks.
Inference pipelines over Hub models without building custom model loading code.
ML experiments that need to pivot across training backends (DeepSpeed, FSDP) or inference backends (vLLM, TGI).
avoid_when: The application only calls hosted LLM APIs and does not need local model loading.
Deployment environment cannot support heavy Python ML dependencies (torch, transformers, etc.).
best_practices: Use pipeline() for quick inference experiments; switch to model + tokenizer for production control.
Set device_map='auto' for automatic multi-GPU/CPU offload when loading large models.
Use BitsAndBytesConfig for 4-bit/8-bit quantization to reduce GPU memory requirements.
Use Trainer with gradient_checkpointing=True and mixed_precision='bf16' for efficient fine-tuning.
Always review model licenses on the Hub before deploying in production; not all are permissive.
Pin model revisions (commit hash) in production to prevent silent model updates.

stacks.name: Qdrant
strengths: AI-native vector search engine built for production: HNSW indexing with high recall and low latency.
Vector quantization (scalar, product, binary) for significant memory reduction with minimal recall loss.
Multi-tenancy support: per-tenant isolated namespaces for SaaS workloads.
Distributed deployment for billion-scale vector search across shards.
Named vectors allow multiple embedding models per document in a single collection.
Rich payload support with filtering: search vectors filtered by structured metadata.
Python qdrant-client, REST, and gRPC interfaces; local in-memory mode for development.
tradeoffs: Self-hosted deployment requires Qdrant infrastructure management and monitoring.
HNSW index requires all vectors in memory for full performance; plan capacity for vector size times collection size.
Cloud offering newer than competitors; evaluate SLA requirements for production workloads.
best_for: Production vector search with quantization, multi-tenancy, and named vectors.
High-volume semantic search pipelines requiring distributed deployment at billion-scale.
Projects that need self-hosted vector storage with no data leaving their infrastructure.
avoid_when: A lightweight embedded vector store (ChromaDB) is sufficient for the scale.
The team cannot maintain self-hosted Qdrant or budget for managed cloud.
best_practices: Use scalar quantization as the default first step to reduce memory 4x with minimal recall loss.
Create payload indexes on frequently filtered fields before loading data for fast filtered search.
Use named vectors to store multiple embedding model outputs (e.g., sparse + dense) per document.
Use QdrantClient(location=':memory:') for local tests; switch to URL for integration tests.
Set on_disk=True for payload and vectors when collection exceeds available RAM.
Monitor collection segment state; run optimize for collections with many deleted vectors.

stacks.name: Polars
strengths: Written in Rust; delivers C/C++-level performance with automatic multi-core parallelism.
Lazy evaluation API with query optimizer reduces memory allocations and enables predicate pushdown.
Streaming API processes out-of-core datasets larger than available RAM.
Apache Arrow compatible for zero-copy interop with other Arrow-native tools.
GPU support (NVIDIA, open beta) for further acceleration of DataFrame operations.
SQL interface via pl.SQLContext for teams preferring SQL over DataFrame API.
First-class cloud storage I/O (S3, GCS, Azure) and database connectors.
Hugging Face datasets integration; scan_parquet and scan_csv for lazy file reading.
tradeoffs: API differs significantly from pandas; teams migrating need upfront investment in new idioms.
Lazy API debugging is less intuitive; .collect() is required to materialize results.
GPU support is experimental; not production-ready for all operations.
best_for: Data pipelines requiring high-throughput DataFrame processing with low memory overhead.
ETL, feature engineering, and analytics workloads that outgrow pandas performance limits.
Teams processing datasets larger than RAM using the streaming API.
avoid_when: The team needs the broad pandas ecosystem compatibility (matplotlib, scikit-learn integrations).
Simple low-volume data transforms where pandas familiarity outweighs performance gains.
best_practices: Prefer the lazy API (pl.scan_csv, pl.scan_parquet) and defer .collect() to the last step.
Use predicate pushdown and projection pushdown: filter and select early in the lazy chain.
Use pl.String consistently; avoid mixing Python strings and Polars string types.
Use .with_columns() for vectorized column creation instead of row-wise apply.
Use streaming=True in .collect() for datasets that exceed available RAM.
Use pl.SQLContext for SQL-familiar teams: register DataFrames and query with SQL.

stacks.name: DuckDB
strengths: In-process columnar SQL analytics engine: no server setup, just import and query.
Reads Parquet, CSV, JSON, Apache Arrow, and Iceberg natively without loading into memory.
Runs SQL directly on Pandas and Polars DataFrames without copy.
Cloud storage queries (S3, GCS, Azure Blob) with zero infrastructure setup.
10-100x faster than SQLite for analytical (OLAP) queries on columnar data.
Full SQL support including window functions, lateral joins, list comprehensions, and WITH RECURSIVE.
Zero external dependencies; embeds directly into Python with duckdb.connect().
File-based persistent DB or in-memory for ephemeral analytics sessions.
tradeoffs: Not suitable for high-concurrency OLTP workloads; single-writer model limits write throughput.
No server mode; not appropriate as a shared remote database for multiple services.
For very large distributed datasets, Polars streaming or a data warehouse may be needed.
best_for: Local data exploration, ETL pipelines, and feature engineering over Parquet/CSV files.
Analysts and data engineers who want SQL over files and DataFrames without a database server.
Lightweight analytical queries embedded in Python applications or notebooks.
avoid_when: The workload requires concurrent transactional writes or shared multi-service access.
Data is already in a hosted data warehouse and DuckDB would duplicate infrastructure.
best_practices: Use duckdb.connect(':memory:') for ephemeral sessions; duckdb.connect('file.db') for persistence.
Use duckdb.read_parquet('s3://bucket/path/*.parquet') for S3 Parquet scans without boto3 setup.
Use duckdb.sql('SELECT * FROM df') to query Polars or pandas DataFrames directly by variable name.
Use COPY TO for efficient bulk export to Parquet, CSV, or JSON formats.
Prefer DuckDB over SQLite for any analytical query involving aggregations on large datasets.
Use EXPLAIN ANALYZE to inspect query plans and identify scan/join bottlenecks.

stacks.name: Terraform
strengths: Write/Plan/Apply workflow: declare desired state in HCL, preview changes with plan, then apply.
Providers for AWS, Azure, GCP, Kubernetes, GitHub, Datadog, Splunk, and thousands more via Terraform Registry.
State file tracks real infrastructure as source of truth for drift detection and dependency resolution.
Modules enable reusable, composable infrastructure components shareable across teams.
Parallel resource creation via dependency graph: independent resources provisioned simultaneously.
HCP Terraform for team collaboration: remote state, policy enforcement, and audit logging.
Immutable infrastructure approach: replace rather than upgrade reduces snowflake configuration drift.
tradeoffs: State file is sensitive; requires secure remote backend (S3+DynamoDB, HCP Terraform) for team use.
Provider ecosystem quality varies; some providers lag behind cloud provider API releases.
Plan/apply cycle adds latency compared to direct SDK calls; not suitable for imperative provisioning logic.
best_for: Multi-cloud or hybrid cloud infrastructure declared as version-controlled code.
Teams that need infrastructure drift detection, plan-before-apply safety, and audit trails.
Provisioning repeatable environments (dev/staging/prod) from shared module libraries.
avoid_when: Infrastructure changes are highly dynamic or require complex imperative logic (use Pulumi or CDK).
A single cloud provider with strong managed IaC tooling reduces cognitive overhead for the team.
best_practices: Always run terraform plan before apply in CI/CD; never apply without reviewing the plan output.
Store state in a remote backend (S3+DynamoDB or HCP Terraform); never commit state files to git.
Use workspaces or directory-per-environment patterns for dev/staging/prod isolation.
Pin provider versions in required_providers to prevent breaking changes from provider updates.
Use terraform fmt and terraform validate in pre-commit hooks for consistent HCL style.
Use modules for reusable patterns; publish to internal module registry for team-wide reuse.

stacks.name: Supabase
strengths: Full-stack platform: PostgreSQL + Auth + Storage + Realtime + Edge Functions + pgvector in one service.
Row Level Security (RLS) on all PostgreSQL tables provides per-row access control at the database layer.
Auth module supports email/password, OAuth (GitHub, Google, etc.), magic link, and mobile providers.
Realtime subscriptions to PostgreSQL change events; broadcast and presence channels.
Storage with RLS access policies for file and media management.
AI & Vectors module: pgvector integration with built-in embedding generation and similarity search via REST.
Queues (pgmq) and Cron (pg_cron) modules for async jobs and scheduled tasks without separate infrastructure.
REST API and GraphQL API auto-generated from schema; Python, JS, Flutter, Swift, Kotlin clients.
Self-hostable via Docker for data residency and compliance requirements.
tradeoffs: Supabase client libraries abstract direct PostgreSQL access; complex queries may need raw SQL via RPC.
Self-hosting requires maintaining multiple interconnected Supabase services.
Vendor lock-in risk: while PostgreSQL is portable, Supabase Auth and Storage have proprietary APIs.
best_for: Full-stack web and mobile apps needing auth, database, storage, and real-time without separate services.
Rapid product prototyping where a managed PostgreSQL backend reduces infrastructure overhead.
Teams that need pgvector AI search integrated directly with relational data and RLS policies.
avoid_when: Complex relational schemas require direct PostgreSQL tooling not exposed by Supabase abstractions.
Data residency requirements conflict with available Supabase cloud regions and self-hosting overhead is prohibitive.
best_practices: Enable RLS on every table from the start; disable only for internal service tables with explicit justification.
Use supabase-py with the anon key for public client access; service_role key only in secure server contexts.
Define database changes as SQL migrations in supabase/migrations/ for reproducible schema evolution.
Use Supabase local dev (supabase start) for consistent local development and migration testing.
Use pgvector via the AI & Vectors module for semantic search embedded directly in PostgreSQL RLS policies.
Never expose the service_role key to client-side code; always use per-user JWT tokens for client auth.

stacks.name: pre-commit
strengths: Multi-language pre-commit hook manager: one tool coordinates hooks across all project languages.
.pre-commit-config.yaml declares repos, hooks, and revision pins for reproducible hook configuration.
pre-commit install sets up git hooks; pre-commit run --all-files for CI full-repo validation.
pre-commit autoupdate keeps hook revisions current with upstream releases.
Hook stages cover pre-commit, pre-push, commit-msg, prepare-commit-msg, and post-checkout.
Local hooks for repo-specific scripts without publishing a separate hook package.
CI integration: GitHub Actions, GitLab, CircleCI, and Azure Pipelines all have pre-commit patterns.
tradeoffs: Adds per-commit latency; slow hooks (mypy, full test suite) should be moved to pre-push or CI only.
Hook environment isolation requires each hook to install its dependencies; initial setup downloads are slow.
Bypassing with --no-verify is always possible; needs CI enforcement to be a hard gate.
best_for: Enforcing code quality, formatting, and linting consistently across all contributors.
Preventing common issues (trailing whitespace, YAML syntax errors, secrets) before they reach CI.
Large codebases with multiple languages needing unified hook management.
avoid_when: The team has strong CI-only quality gates and local hooks add friction without clear benefit.
Hooks are so slow they frequently get bypassed with --no-verify, defeating the purpose.
best_practices: Pin hook revisions to tags (rev: v0.x.y) in .pre-commit-config.yaml; run autoupdate in CI quarterly.
Keep pre-commit hooks fast (Ruff, trailing-whitespace, check-yaml); move slow checks (mypy, tests) to pre-push.
Run pre-commit run --all-files in CI as a required status check to prevent hook bypass drift.
Use the standard hooks repo (pre-commit/pre-commit-hooks) for trailing-whitespace, end-of-file-fixer, check-yaml, check-json.
Add detect-secrets or gitleaks hook to prevent accidental credential commits.
Use stages: [pre-push] on expensive hooks to balance developer experience with quality enforcement.

stacks.name: mypy
strengths: Official Python static type checker; catches type errors before runtime across entire codebases.
--strict flag enables all optional error checks: disallow_untyped_defs, disallow_any_generics, warn_return_any, etc.
Protocol support for structural subtyping: define interfaces without inheritance.
TypeGuard/TypeIs for custom type narrowing functions in isinstance-style patterns.
TypedDict for typed dictionary schemas; Literal types for restricted value sets.
Generics with TypeVar, ParamSpec, and TypeVarTuple for type-safe higher-order functions.
Incremental mode caches results for fast re-checks; mypy daemon (dmypy) for persistent background checking.
Per-module configuration allows gradual adoption: strict on new modules, lenient on legacy code.
tradeoffs: Full --strict adoption on an existing untyped codebase requires significant annotation investment.
Some dynamic Python patterns (metaprogramming, heavy use of **kwargs) resist clean type annotation.
False positives in complex generic code may require type: ignore suppressions with error codes.
best_for: Large Python codebases where type errors across module boundaries are a common failure mode.
Libraries and frameworks where public API contracts must be statically verifiable by consumers.
Teams adopting gradual typing: start permissive, tighten per-module over time.
avoid_when: Small scripts or notebooks where type annotation overhead exceeds the benefit.
Heavy dynamic Python metaclass patterns that fundamentally resist static analysis.
best_practices: Configure mypy in pyproject.toml under [tool.mypy]; set python_version and enable strict mode per module.
Start with strict = false and enable checks incrementally: disallow_untyped_defs first, then warn_return_any.
Use # type: ignore[error-code] for unavoidable suppressions; never use bare # type: ignore.
Use Protocol for structural interfaces rather than ABCs when inheritance is not semantically meaningful.
Use dmypy for fast incremental checks in development; run full mypy in CI on the complete codebase.
Use from __future__ import annotations for forward references and improved annotation performance.
Add mypy to pre-commit hooks with stages: [pre-push] to avoid per-commit latency.

integration_patterns.name: Server-rendered product monolith
notes: Choose when a single team can own product, data, auth, admin, and operational workflows together.
Prefer over premature service decomposition when speed and governance matter more than independent deployment.

integration_patterns.name: SPA plus API
notes: Keep the backend API as the system contract and let the SPA stay deployment-independent.
Use when SSR and BFF behavior add little product value.

integration_patterns.name: SSR or BFF web platform
notes: Choose when route rendering, mutation, caching, and auth should be designed together.
Set strict boundaries between server components, route handlers, and external services early.

integration_patterns.name: API plus background workers
notes: Keep request handlers thin and move non-immediate work to jobs.
Do not treat queues as durable workflow state unless the job model truly stays short-lived.

integration_patterns.name: Durable workflow platform
notes: Choose when retries, waits, timers, and state transitions must be visible and durable.
Prefer over ad hoc chained jobs when the process itself is a product concern.

integration_patterns.name: Hybrid retrieval system
notes: Keep source-of-truth metadata and relations structured, then layer lexical and semantic retrieval over them.
Do not let embeddings become the authority model.

integration_patterns.name: LLM-powered agent platform
components: OpenAI API or Anthropic Claude API
notes: Use function calling or MCP tool servers for structured agent actions.
Validate all LLM outputs through Pydantic models before executing side effects.
Start with deterministic tool orchestration before adding agentic autonomy.

integration_patterns.name: RAG pipeline with evaluation
notes: Follow the five RAG stages: loading, indexing, storing, querying, evaluation.
Measure retrieval quality separately from generation quality.
Optimize in order: prompt engineering, then RAG retrieval tuning, then fine-tuning only if needed.

integration_patterns.name: Python CI/CD quality pipeline
notes: Ruff replaces flake8+isort+black as a single fast linter/formatter.
Use GitHub Actions matrix builds for multi-Python-version testing.
Pin action versions by SHA, use OIDC for cloud auth, and never interpolate secrets in run scripts.

integration_patterns.name: Security-hardened API service
notes: Apply OWASP API Security Top 10 as a design review checklist, not just a post-hoc audit.
Use Pydantic strict mode for input validation at API boundaries.
Implement object-level authorization checks in every data access path.
Use Argon2id/bcrypt for password hashing; require MFA for sensitive operations.
Set security headers (HSTS, X-Content-Type-Options, CSP) on every response.

integration_patterns.name: Async Python microservice
notes: Use asyncio TaskGroup for structured concurrency: automatic cancellation on failure, ExceptionGroup propagation.
Use asyncio.to_thread for blocking I/O calls (file operations, legacy sync libraries).
Use logger = logging.getLogger(__name__) for hierarchical log namespacing across modules.
Use LoggerAdapter to inject request-scoped context (request_id, trace_id) into all log entries.

integration_patterns.name: Type-safe Python application
notes: Use Protocol for structural interfaces, TypeVar with bounds for generic functions, ParamSpec for decorator typing.
Use frozen=True dataclasses for value objects; slots=True for memory optimization.
Use Pydantic for external input validation at API boundaries; dataclasses for internal data containers.
Use Hypothesis property-based testing for data transformation invariants and serialization round-trips.
Run mypy or pyright in CI alongside Ruff for comprehensive static analysis.

integration_patterns.name: AI agent with prompt discipline
components: OpenAI API or Anthropic Claude API
notes: Use output contracts, verification loops, and completeness contracts for reliable multi-step execution.
Start with reasoning_effort: none/low for execution-heavy tasks; medium/high for research-heavy; tune by eval results.
Use strict mode for function calls and structured outputs; validate all LLM outputs through Pydantic before acting.
Implement tool persistence rules with dependency checks to prevent prerequisite skipping.
Use research mode (plan-retrieve-synthesize) for multi-source evidence gathering workflows.

integration_patterns.name: ML inference pipeline
notes: Use Polars for high-throughput data preprocessing and feature extraction before model inference.
Serve inference via FastAPI with structured Pydantic response models; instrument all calls with OpenTelemetry.
Pin model revisions by commit hash in production; review licenses before deployment.

integration_patterns.name: Full-spectrum LLM multi-agent system
notes: Use LiteLLM as the unified gateway for provider flexibility, cost tracking, and fallback routing.
Use Instructor for structured extraction with automatic validation retry across all providers.
Use LangGraph for stateful multi-step agent graphs with checkpointing and human-in-the-loop gates.
Expose tools via MCP servers for standardized discovery across agent hosts.
Validate all LLM outputs through Pydantic before executing side effects.
Store agent checkpoints and conversation state in PostgreSQL; instrument with OpenTelemetry.

decision_rules.title: Keep authoritative structure above retrieval ranking
decision_rules.when: The product needs recommendations, context lookup, or RAG answers that affect planning or execution order.
decision_rules.prefer: Canonical docs, registry state, typed KB relations, and explicit ownership edges as the source of truth, with lexical/vector retrieval used only for ranking.
decision_rules.because: Authority, sequence, and dependency decisions degrade when embeddings or generic summaries become the truth model.
avoid: Letting vector similarity decide canonical ownership.
Flattening tradeoffs, authority, and evidence into a single narrative blob.

decision_rules.title: Escalate from task queues to durable workflows on stateful process boundaries
decision_rules.when: The process includes retries, timers, compensation, approvals, or human waits across multiple steps.
decision_rules.prefer: Temporal or another durable workflow system instead of stretched queue chains.
decision_rules.because: Long-running stateful business processes need replayable state and visible transitions, not opaque task graphs.
avoid: Modeling multi-day approval flows as chained Celery tasks.
Keeping workflow state only in broker payloads.

decision_rules.title: Prefer Python 3.14 for new async and tooling-heavy services after dependency validation
decision_rules.when: You are starting a new Python service or planning a runtime upgrade and your dependency matrix is green on 3.14.
decision_rules.prefer: Python 3.14 for new concurrency-heavy services, tooling-forward platforms, and runtime research work.
decision_rules.because: 3.14 makes free-threaded mode supported, adds stdlib subinterpreter primitives, improves asyncio observability, and changes annotation evaluation in ways worth adopting deliberately.
avoid: Blind upgrades before validating wheels, CI images, and runtime annotation consumers.
Using free-threaded mode as the default before workload-specific benchmarking.

decision_rules.title: Run weekly deep research as delta maintenance, not open-ended exploration
decision_rules.when: A knowledge domain changes quickly enough to affect stack, architecture, or operational recommendations.
decision_rules.prefer: A fixed weekly review cadence with explicit outputs: change detected, recommendation changed or unchanged, evidence links, and KB update scope.
decision_rules.because: Scheduled research is only valuable if it produces reviewable deltas instead of growing an unstructured archive.
avoid: Appending research notes directly into stack entries without a decision summary.
Mixing speculative signals with shipped production guidance.

relationships.subject: FastAPI
relationships.rationale: FastAPI's boundary validation and OpenAPI generation are strongest when request and response contracts are enforced through typed models.
evidence: FastAPI request validation and schema generation rely on Python type annotations and Pydantic models.

relationships.subject: Temporal
relationships.rationale: Temporal should replace stretched queue chains once a process requires durable state, timers, compensation, or human waits.
evidence: Celery is effective for finite background work but becomes opaque for long-lived workflows.
Temporal keeps state transitions explicit and replayable.

relationships.subject: pgvector
relationships.rationale: pgvector fits when vectors should stay near relational authority, while ChromaDB is better for fast prototyping or smaller standalone retrieval systems.
evidence: The choice depends on whether relational authority or isolated vector iteration is the primary constraint.

relationships.subject: Python Asyncio
relationships.rationale: Async systems become hard to operate without request and task-level logging context.
evidence: Task-aware logging and request-scoped context reduce debugging cost in concurrent services.

relationships.subject: Python 3.14
relationships.rationale: For new services with validated dependencies, 3.14 is the stronger default because it upgrades concurrency and runtime tooling from experimental or partial to more operationally usable surfaces.
evidence: 3.13 introduced experimental free-threaded CPython and JIT work.
3.14 supports free-threaded mode and adds stdlib multiple-interpreter tooling.

relationships.subject: FastAPI
relationships.rationale: FastAPI is the better default when the primary surface is a typed API or automation service, while Django is stronger when the product needs an integrated admin, forms, and server-rendered workflow stack.
evidence: FastAPI minimizes ceremony around typed APIs and async service boundaries.
Django provides batteries-included platform features that reduce integration choices for business systems.

relationships.subject: pgvector
relationships.rationale: pgvector is strongest when vectors should stay near relational authority and transactional metadata.
evidence: Relational and vector data can share one operational boundary when the system of record is PostgreSQL.

comparisons.name: Python 3.13 vs 3.14 for production application platforms
comparisons.summary: Python 3.13 introduced important runtime experiments and typing improvements, while 3.14 turns more of that surface into practical platform guidance with supported free-threaded builds, stdlib subinterpreter tooling, annotation evaluation changes, and better operational debugging hooks.

findings.summary: 3.13 introduced experimental free-threaded CPython; 3.14 makes free-threaded mode officially supported and adds stdlib multiple-interpreter tooling such as InterpreterPoolExecutor.
findings.option_a: 3.13: useful for early benchmarking and compatibility scouting, but not the strongest default for production concurrency decisions.
findings.option_b: 3.14: stronger default for new concurrency-heavy systems once dependencies and deployment images are validated.
findings.recommendation: Use 3.14 for new async and concurrency research work; keep 3.13 where dependency support or deployment base images lag.
findings.migration_risk: Medium: verify native extensions, worker models, and runtime assumptions before upgrading.

findings.summary: 3.13 expanded typing features such as defaults, ReadOnly, and TypeIs; 3.14 changes annotation evaluation semantics through deferred evaluation, which can affect runtime introspection and libraries that inspect annotations eagerly.
findings.option_a: 3.13: safer if your framework or internal code relies on current eager annotation behavior.
findings.option_b: 3.14: better long-term default if you validate runtime annotation consumers and want the newer semantics.
findings.recommendation: Treat 3.14 annotation changes as an explicit migration checkpoint for FastAPI, Pydantic, and internal reflection code.
findings.migration_risk: Medium to high for codebases that depend on runtime annotation evaluation details.
